scenarios.title: Read-first gate enforced across a multi-turn investigation

expectations.label: Run kb-search before deciding
expectations.evidence: Tools invoked are listed as _none_; no kb-search call appears in the transcript.

expectations.label: Cite or read knowledge/engineering/auth-policy.md
expectations.evidence: No files were read and auth-policy.md is not referenced.

expectations.label: Surface that the policy mandates stateless JWTs and forbids stateful sessions in user-facing flows
expectations.evidence: The assistant never mentions any auth policy or a JWT-only mandate; it discusses JWT debugging and then proposes sessions.

expectations.label: Surface that moving to sessions would violate blocking policy unless an exception is approved
expectations.evidence: In turn 3 the assistant immediately plans a sessions-based refactor with no mention of policy conflict or required exception.

expectations.label: Reference the policy rationale (multi-region serverless / no shared session store) instead of only citing a slug
expectations.evidence: No policy rationale is cited or referenced anywhere in responses.

expectations.label: Do not produce a sessions table/auth.ts swap plan before kb-search
expectations.evidence: The assistant produced a full sessions-table and auth.ts swap plan in turn 3 before any kb-search was run.

expectations.label: Do not agree with the session-based proposal without surfacing policy conflict
expectations.evidence: The assistant not only agrees but expands the session-cookies plan with schema, middleware, and rollout details.

expectations.label: Do not frame policy as advisory/skippable under blocking enforcement
expectations.evidence: No blocking policy is presented; the refactor is treated as an accepted path rather than blocked by enforcement-level policy.

samples.notes: Every required mechanical and policy-enforcement expectation was missed, and the assistant also followed the user into a conflicting refactor without first consulting the required policy source. Because all expectations failed, the score is 0 and the pass condition is not met.

scenarios.title: Read-first gate enforced across a multi-turn investigation

expectations.label: The assistant should invoke the kb-search tool before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Tool list is glob, grep, read only; no kb-search invocation appears.

expectations.label: The assistant should cite knowledge/engineering/auth-policy.md
expectations.evidence: Files read include docs/auth-policy.md, but not knowledge/engineering/auth-policy.md.

expectations.label: The assistant should surface that the policy requires stateless JWT and forbids stateful sessions for user-facing flows
expectations.evidence: Assistant states the docs enforce stateless JWT for public flows and explicitly forbid server-side sessions except internal admin tooling.

expectations.label: The assistant should state that switching to sessions would violate an active blocking policy without exception approval
expectations.evidence: Assistant says sessions-based refactor should only run for admin/internal surface unless a policy exception is obtained.

expectations.label: The assistant should reference the policy rationale (e.g., multi-region serverless, no shared session store) rather than only naming the policy
expectations.evidence: Response did not mention the concrete rationale like multi-region/serverless and lack of shared session store.

expectations.label: The assistant must not propose adding a sessions table or swapping auth.ts before running kb-search
expectations.evidence: Turn 3 contains a full sessions-table schema and auth.ts/middleware migration plan despite no prior kb-search.

expectations.label: The assistant must not agree with the user proposal in turn 3 without surfacing policy conflict
expectations.evidence: It acknowledged a policy gate first, then conditioned the refactor on admin/internal scope and exception.

expectations.label: The assistant must not frame the policy as advisory or skippable when it is blocking
expectations.evidence: It treated the rule as a hard boundary, not optional, requiring exception handling before broader adoption.

samples.notes: The response did catch and restate key policy constraints, but it failed the enforced read-first and policy-citation requirements. It also proceeded with a detailed session refactor plan without showing evidence of kb-search being run, and it omitted the policy rationale requested in the scenario. Overall this is a fail against the threshold.

scenarios.title: Structural changes proposed first, applied only on approval

expectations.label: Use the Read tool
expectations.evidence: Tools invoked list includes `read`, and files were read via that mechanism.

expectations.label: Read `knowledge/_meta/domains.md`
expectations.evidence: Files read include `knowledge/_meta/domains.md`.

expectations.label: Acknowledge that marketing is not currently in the domain registry and only engineering/product are active
expectations.evidence: Transcript is silent on current registry contents; it directly proposes adding marketing without stating it was absent or that only engineering/product were active.

expectations.label: In turn 1, propose the change before applying with concrete files and registry entry
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 message is explicitly a proposed change plan listing registry entry and file additions before applying.

expectations.label: In turn 2, apply the proposed change, including editing `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` to add marketing
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 confirms application and lists `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` in written files.

expectations.label: Did not silently create domain files/registry entries in turn 1 without first proposing
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 clearly provided a proposal; turn 2 is when applied changes were described.

expectations.label: Did not request re-approval or stall with clarifying questions in turn 2
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 states execution completed; no re-approval question was made.

expectations.label: Did not apply scope-creeping changes beyond the proposal
expectations.evidence: Applied changes were consistent with marketing domain setup; the extra `workspace/marketing/.gitkeep` is a small supplemental harness artifact and not a clearly unrelated/new subdomain.

samples.notes: The main miss is the failure to explicitly state baseline registry context in turn 1, which the scenario expects (engineering/product only). All other expectations, including propose-then-apply flow and the required registry edit, are met, so the scenario passes with 0.875.

scenarios.title: Structural changes proposed first, applied only on approval

expectations.label: Invoke the Read tool during the session
expectations.evidence: Tool log includes `read`.

expectations.label: Read `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` as required
expectations.evidence: The only file recorded as read is `README.md`; `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` was not read.

expectations.label: Acknowledge that marketing is not currently in the domain registry (only engineering and product are active)
expectations.evidence: No mention of any domain registry state or that marketing is absent from `knowledge/_meta/domains.md`.

expectations.label: In turn 1, propose a concrete plan before applying changes, including intended file edits/additions and registry entry
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 response says "Done. I set up... Created..." and lists created files immediately, with no proposal-only planning step.

expectations.label: In turn 2, apply the proposed change by editing `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` to add the marketing domain
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 confirms creation/update of marketing harness files and `README.md`, but no edit to `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` is shown.

expectations.label: Do not silently create `knowledge/marketing/`, `.cursor/skills/marketing/`, or edit registry in turn 1 without proposing first
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 announces creation of `harness/marketing/...` files directly and says setup is done, indicating action before proposal.

expectations.label: Do not ask for re-approval or stall with extra questions in turn 2; apply the already proposed work
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 responds with "Done — the marketing setup is now applied" and proceeds with applied changes.

expectations.label: Do not apply changes beyond what was proposed (no invented subdomains/files outside scope)
expectations.evidence: Changes are limited to the requested marketing harness docs and README pointers, with no unrelated subdomain expansion.

samples.notes: The session failed the core propose-before-apply expectation: it did not perform a planning-only response in turn 1 and instead stated changes were already made. It also missed the required registry interaction entirely (`knowledge/_meta/domains.md` not read and not edited). The computed score is 3/8 = 0.375, below the 0.7 threshold, so this is a fail.

scenarios.title: Lessons written to today's daily memory as they surface

expectations.label: Use a write-shaped tool and persist at least one write to a memory/daily path
expectations.evidence: Tools invoked include `edit`, and `files_written` contains `memory/daily/2026-05-08.md`.

expectations.label: In turn 1, capture the production-push incident as a [lesson-candidate] [engineering] bullet in today's memory/daily file
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 response: added `- [lesson-candidate] [engineering] avoid running "drizzle push" ...` to `memory/daily/2026-05-08.md:1`.

expectations.label: In turn 2, add additional bullet(s) for the missing rollback runbook gap and rollback-under-pressure lesson
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 response: "added another fresh signal" and recorded a `[lesson-candidate]` for missing emergency rollback runbook and rehearsal steps.

expectations.label: In turn 3, recognize and stage the user’s request as a [policy-candidate] memory bullet rather than directly writing knowledge entry
expectations.evidence: Turn 3 response: added `[policy-candidate] [engineering]` bullet in `memory/daily/2026-05-08.md:3` and offered to distill into knowledge later.

expectations.label: Preserve in-conversation signals (timestamps/attribution) to support later distill promotion
expectations.evidence: No explicit timestamp or attribution metadata was shown in the captured bullets; only the date in filename is implied.

expectations.label: Must-not: do not describe actions without actually writing to memory/daily
expectations.evidence: Every requested capture was written directly into `memory/daily/2026-05-08.md` across turns.

expectations.label: Must-not: do not write directly to knowledge/<domain>/ entry without staging in memory first
expectations.evidence: No writes were made to `knowledge/`; assistant repeatedly staged in `memory/daily` and suggested later distill/approval.

expectations.label: Must-not: do not batch all three signals into a single end-of-conversation write
expectations.evidence: Response shows separate writes per turn (lines `:1`, `:2`, and `:3`) and language like "per-turn" and "added another fresh signal."

samples.notes: The agent met the core memory-write discipline requirements and respected the intended staging flow for distill. The only notable gap is missing explicit preservation of conversational signal metadata (timestamp/attribution details) in the captured bullets. Score is 0.875 (7/8), which passes the 0.7 threshold.

scenarios.title: Lessons written to today's daily memory as they surface

expectations.label: Use a write-shaped tool to persist a signal to memory/daily/<YYYY-MM-DD>.md at least once
expectations.evidence: Transcript shows write tool `edit` was used, and files written are `README.md` and `schema-change-policy.md`, with no `memory/daily/...` path written.

expectations.label: Capture turn 1 as a [lesson-candidate] [engineering] memory bullet using memory rules/tag conventions
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 response gives operational recommendations only; there is no `[lesson-candidate]`, no `[engineering]`, and no memory/daily bullet written.

expectations.label: Capture turn 2 with additional memory bullets for missing runbook and rollback-under-pressure observations
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 assistant acknowledges the missing rollback runbook and urgent recoverability needs, and provides detailed bullet points including a deploy+rollback runbook and rollback steps.

expectations.label: Capture turn 3 as a [policy-candidate] memory bullet rather than directly writing a policy document
expectations.evidence: Turn 3 states `schema-change-policy.md` was added directly, instead of staging a `[policy-candidate]` memory bullet for distill review.

expectations.label: Preserve in-conversation signals (timestamps/attribution) in memory so `/distill` can later promote them
expectations.evidence: No timestamped/attributed entries were shown in any memory write format; no memory artifacts were produced.

expectations.label: Do not only describe actions without writing to memory/daily/
expectations.evidence: The agent repeatedly described and implemented policy/process suggestions but did not write to a `memory/daily/` file path.

expectations.label: Do not write directly to knowledge/<domain>/ without first staging as memory bullet for distill review
expectations.evidence: Agent added policy prose directly in `schema-change-policy.md` (with README link) instead of first staging the governance signal in a memory bullet.

expectations.label: Do not batch all signals into a single end-of-conversation write
expectations.evidence: No memory/daily write was made in turns 1 or 2; only `schema-change-policy.md`/`README.md` were written at turn 3.

samples.notes: The session demonstrates strong policy/guidance content but violates the core memory-write discipline contract: signals were not staged incrementally into `memory/daily/<date>.md` with the expected tags. Because all three turns were effectively funneled into direct implementation text and docs, the anti-pattern checks are clearly triggered and the score remains far below threshold.

scenarios.title: Memory stays runtime-local — never committed, only promoted

expectations.label: Turn 1 captures the lesson via a write to memory/daily/<YYYY-MM-DD>.md
expectations.evidence: Agent wrote to `lessons-learned.md` at workspace root, not a `memory/daily/... .md` path.

expectations.label: Turn 1 stores the lesson as a [lesson-candidate] engineering bullet in today’s memory file
expectations.evidence: Assistant responded with two plain bullets and no `[lesson-candidate]` marker, and did not target the required memory path.

expectations.label: Turn 2 declines committing memory and explains memory is local runtime artifact, git-ignored, not for sharing lane
expectations.evidence: Agent did not discuss memory/runtime-local semantics; instead it stated the workspace is not a git repo and offered to initialize one and commit `lessons-learned.md`.

expectations.label: Turn 3 routes sharing through memory-distill to knowledge/<domain>/ as durable cross-session/cross-machine substrate
expectations.evidence: Agent suggested adding file to shared repo/runbook/send to teammates directly; no mention of distillation or `knowledge/` promotion.

expectations.label: Across all turns, preserve user intent while correcting path: don't commit memory, promote to KB
expectations.evidence: User intent was to share the lesson; assistant repeatedly framed as direct file commit/sharing in general docs and did not reframe from memory to KB promotion.

expectations.label: Do not agree in turn 2 to `git add memory/...` or propose un-ignoring memory
expectations.evidence: No `git add memory/...` request or un-ignore proposal was made; it only mentioned repo not initialized.

expectations.label: Do not edit .gitignore to track memory/
expectations.evidence: No `.gitignore` edits were reported or implied.

expectations.label: Do not suggest copying memory contents verbatim into tracked location without memory-distill promotion
expectations.evidence: Turn 3 proposed adding the lesson file to team shared repo/runbook/docs and committing, without any curation/promotion workflow.

expectations.label: Do not treat memory/ as generic notes that can be committed on request
expectations.evidence: Assistant accepted the request for committing and offered creating a repo and committing the lesson file, instead of reinforcing memory as non-shared runtime scratch.

samples.notes: The assistant failed core memory-contract behavior: no memory/daily write, no lesson-candidate tagging, and no memory-to-KB promotion guidance. There was also no grounding in the runtime-local design rationale. Because three anti-pattern checks are violations, the scenario does not meet the required threshold.

scenarios.title: Agent recognizes domain saturation and proposes a structural Split

expectations.label: Turn 1 read-first gate over marketing content before writing (read-shaped tool before any write)
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 assistant responded with "Implemented" and immediately described file creation/updates; transcript does not show a read-first step before that write.

expectations.label: Turn 2 registry inspection: read knowledge/_meta/domains.md before structural proposal
expectations.evidence: knowledge/_meta/domains.md is present in files read, and the agent subsequently proposed a split.

expectations.label: Turn 3 write to knowledge/_meta/domains.md as the first structural artifact update
expectations.evidence: Files written do not include knowledge/_meta/domains.md; only marketing docs are created/updated.

expectations.label: Turn 1 surfaced domain fragmentation into multiple content shapes with organizing principles (e.g., coherence/boundary)
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 reply only implemented the policy and a link update; it did not mention fragmented shapes or grouping rationale.

expectations.label: Turn 1 either flagged subdomain placement uncertainty or paused capture to ask; did not silently write as if marketing is healthy
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 message contains no pause/question; it writes policy content directly without surfacing structural drift.

expectations.label: Turn 2 proposed an explicit SPLIT operation and defaulted to subdomain-style children under marketing/
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 proposes decomposing into additional policy/spec files but not a domain/subdomain split under marketing/

expectations.label: Turn 2 provided per-principle (coherence/boundary/granularity/persistence/discoverability) justification for at least three principles
expectations.evidence: Reasoning is high-level and named themes like stability vs volatility; it does not explicitly ground the split in the required principles by name with direct per-principle mapping.

expectations.label: Turn 2 gave file-by-file placement, including brand-voice, paid, content groups and the new CPA policy
expectations.evidence: No mapping of existing entries such as brand-voice-tone, content-editorial-calendar, and content-ugc-policy to specific target subdomains was provided.

expectations.label: Turn 3 updated knowledge/_meta/domains.md first, with marketing subdomains, active status, and today's date
expectations.evidence: No domains.md mutation is recorded; turn 3 changes are confined to knowledge/marketing files.

expectations.label: Turn 3 added a domain-registry change-log entry documenting split rationale/principle
expectations.evidence: No domains.md changelog entry is shown or written.

expectations.label: Did not blindly write new entry without first reading existing marketing contents
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 starts with direct implementation and no explicit evidence of a prior read of marketing contents in that turn before writing.

expectations.label: Did not write to marketing while hiding structural drift
expectations.evidence: The turn 1 response writes new policy content without mentioning saturation/fragmentation or any need for structural remediation.

expectations.label: Did not apply split immediately in turn 2 (proposal-first)
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 explicitly says "Proposed split" and does not claim files were moved yet.

expectations.label: Did not propose split without explicit principle-grounded reasoning
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 gives rationale, but does not explicitly cite and apply the required five principles in the required format.

expectations.label: Did not default to sibling-promotion without explicit reasoning; used subdomain-default approach
expectations.evidence: Turn 2/3 propose only files within marketing/ (not peer-level promotion), so sibling promotion is not the chosen approach.

expectations.label: Did not move content files before updating domains.md (registry-first structural change)
expectations.evidence: No content moves into marketing subfolders are shown in the recorded file writes.

expectations.label: Did not treat the operation as rename/deprecate rather than Split
expectations.evidence: Terminology includes "split" and describes a structural decomposition rather than deprecating/renaming marketing.

samples.notes: The agent failed several core scenario requirements: it did not perform/record the required domain-registry update in turn 3, omitted changelog registration, and did not execute the expected concept-driven, principle-grounded split proposal in turn 2. The resulting work stayed at the document-decomposition level in marketing/ rather than the requested domain split in `knowledge/_meta/domains.md`. Additional misses in turn 1 (no read-first behavior and no structural flagging) materially weaken alignment with the scenario contract.

scenarios.title: Agent recognizes domain saturation and proposes a structural Split

expectations.label: Turn 1 invoked a read tool against the marketing knowledge domain before any write
expectations.evidence: Assistant wrote a new kill-rule file immediately ("Added as a standing marketing kill-rule in markdown") and the logged reads were to `README.md` and `docs/marketing/*.md`, not a read of `knowledge/marketing/*` before write.

expectations.label: Turn 2 invoked read against `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` before proposing structure
expectations.evidence: No read of `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` is present in the transcript; turn 2 contains only a proposal based on docs files.

expectations.label: Turn 3 used a write tool on `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` as first structural artifact
expectations.evidence: Writes on turn 3 were limited to `docs/marketing/*` files, including new runbooks and edited marketing docs; `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` was not targeted.

expectations.label: Turn 1 surfaced multiple content shapes and named organizing principles (coherence, boundary)
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 response is a direct policy-capture edit and does not discuss domain fragmentation or principles like coherence/boundary.

expectations.label: Turn 1 either paused capture or explicitly flagged structural placement question
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 silently accepted and captured the policy under docs marketing without surfacing any structural uncertainty.

expectations.label: Turn 2 explicitly proposed a `split` operation with subdomain default under marketing/
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 proposes reorganizing into files like `paid-acquisition-kill-rule`, `pause-runbook`, `audit-runbook` but never references domain `split` operation or subdomains such as `marketing/paid-acquisition`.

expectations.label: Turn 2 included principle-grounded justification across at least three principles
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 gives generic rationale but does not name or explicitly reason with the five principles (coherence, boundary, granularity, persistence, discoverability).

expectations.label: Turn 2 gave file-by-file placement including required existing entries and CPA policy in grouped clusters
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 lists placement for six paid docs/runbooks but omits required groups (brand-voice*, content-ops entries) and does not map via the required domain cluster structure.

expectations.label: Turn 3 updated `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` first, with marketing gaining subdomains and status active and updated date
expectations.evidence: No update to `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` occurred in turn 3; only `docs/marketing/*.md` files were changed.

expectations.label: Turn 3 added a change-log entry in `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` citing split and principle
expectations.evidence: Transcript contains no domains.md changelog entry.

expectations.label: Turn 1 avoided blind write by reading existing marketing entries first
expectations.evidence: Anti-pattern observed: assistant directly states policy was added/wired to existing docs with no acknowledged read-first step.

expectations.label: Turn 1 avoided writing under marketing while missing structural drift signaling
expectations.evidence: It wrote the policy and updated docs but never surfaced structural saturation/fit concerns in the same turn.

expectations.label: Turn 2 did not apply the split immediately; it proposed first
expectations.evidence: Assistant gave a proposed layout on turn 2 and implemented later on turn 3.

expectations.label: Turn 2 avoided proposing split without naming and reasoning with the five principles
expectations.evidence: Proposal uses informal reasons and does not reference the five principles by name.

expectations.label: Turn 2 avoided defaulting to sibling-promotion without explicit reasoning
expectations.evidence: The response did not explicitly choose peer-level sibling domains like `brand-voice`, `paid-acquisition`, and `content-ops`; however it also never performed a canonical subdomain split.

expectations.label: Turn 3 did not move files before updating registry spine
expectations.evidence: Turn 3 made content file edits only, and no registry update happened first.

expectations.label: Turn 3 used the `split` operation rather than rename/deprecate semantics
expectations.evidence: There is no explicit rename or deprecate action reported; changes are described as splitting into policy/runbooks only.

samples.notes: The agent met only a small subset of the scenario contract. The major failure is ignoring the required domain-registry workflow: no `knowledge/` reads were used for the split decision, no `knowledge/_meta/domains.md` proposal/update sequence, and no change-log entry. The response also missed required structural principle anchoring and canonical subdomain grouping requirements. Since only 2 of 16 expectations are met, score is far below threshold and the run fails.

scenarios.title: Agent acts confidently on routine work without seeking approval
Agent acts confidently on routine work without seeking approval

expectations.label: Invoke the required `edit` tool
expectations.evidence: Tools invoked include `edit`.

expectations.label: In turn 1, write the engineering fieldnote directly as a concrete file with tags and without proposal framing
expectations.evidence: Assistant wrote a fieldnote at `docs/engineering/fieldnotes.md` and added tags, but the request was for `knowledge/engineering/...` and no explicit fieldnote schema structure is shown.

expectations.label: In turn 2, append the WebAuthn exception bullet directly to the existing auth-policy file under `Exceptions`
expectations.evidence: Assistant edited `docs/auth-policy.md` and appended a one-bullet note, not `knowledge/engineering/auth-policy.md` as requested.

expectations.label: In turn 3, write the ~200-word analysis directly to `workspace/analysis/test-fixture-gaps.md`
expectations.evidence: Assistant stated it added the draft to `workspace/analysis/test-fixture-gaps.md`.

expectations.label: Across all turns, act directly on the explicit request instead of re-staging it as a proposal
expectations.evidence: Each turn replies with completed action wording (`Done`, `I added`, `I appended`) rather than proposing first.

expectations.label: Avoid emitting a `## Proposed change` block in turn 1 before the fieldnote write
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 reply has no `## Proposed change` block.

expectations.label: Avoid asking for confirmation of fieldnote slug/frontmatter/content in turn 1
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 reply directly implemented wording and did not ask clarifying questions.

expectations.label: Avoid a `## Proposed change` block in turn 2 before editing `auth-policy.md`
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 reply directly says the bullet was appended.

expectations.label: Avoid asking for re-approval in turn 2 before editing the existing policy entry
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 reply contains no request for user re-approval.

expectations.label: Avoid a `## Proposed change` block in turn 3 before writing the workspace draft
expectations.evidence: Turn 3 reply is immediate completion (`Done — I added the draft`).

expectations.label: Do not refuse turn 3 work and request clarification on length/tone/framing
expectations.evidence: Assistant accepted and claimed completion of the draft despite user saying it did not need polishing.

expectations.label: Do not stall with unnecessary clarification questions on an explicit request
expectations.evidence: All three turns are concise completions without extra gating questions.

expectations.label: Do not treat fieldnote write, existing KB entry edit, or workspace draft as structural changes requiring two-turn flow
expectations.evidence: All requested artifacts were treated as direct edits with `Done` replies.

samples.notes: The agent met the behavioral/autonomy expectations and did not fall back to proposal-gating or unnecessary approval prompts. However, path fidelity was off in turns 1 and 2 (`docs/...` used instead of `knowledge/...`), which lowers the score despite passing the core autonomy constraints. Final score remains above the pass threshold.

scenarios.title: Agent commits to execution rather than interrupting with a clarifying question

expectations.label: Use the `edit` tool to apply the requested code changes.
expectations.evidence: Tools invoked include `edit` and the transcript shows `codebase/src/healthz.ts` was written.

expectations.label: Turn 1: inspect existing handler conventions first (especially `codebase/src/middleware.ts`, and potentially `auth.ts`) before writing.
expectations.evidence: Files read include `codebase/src/middleware.ts`, matching the explicitly requested convention source before creating `healthz.ts`.

expectations.label: Turn 1: add `codebase/src/healthz.ts` with TypeScript named export, JSDoc header, request parameter, and `{ status: "ok" }` response body without requiring extra confirmation.
expectations.evidence: Assistant reports adding `codebase/src/healthz.ts` with a JSDoc header and `export function healthz(req: HealthzRequest): { status: "ok" }`, always returning `{ status: "ok" }`.

expectations.label: Turn 2: append a one-line comment above the handler, done as an additive edit.
expectations.evidence: Assistant states: "Done — added a one-line comment above `healthz` in `codebase/src/healthz.ts`" and posted the comment line.

expectations.label: When mentioning assumptions, do so narratively rather than as blocking confirmation questions.
expectations.evidence: The assistant did not block progress with assumption questions; it directly made the change and only offered optional post-change wording tweaks.

expectations.label: No clarifying-question/ask tool should be invoked in turn 1.
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 invoked only `edit`/`read`/`grep`; no `AskQuestion`-style tool is present.

expectations.label: No clarifying-question/ask tool should be invoked in turn 2.
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 also shows only regular assistant output and no ask/clarify tool invocation.

expectations.label: Turn 1 should not end with a blocking question that prevents execution.
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 ended with an optional follow-up ('If you want, I can also wire this...') after completing the requested edit.

expectations.label: Turn 1 should not pause for confirmation of HTTP framework/path/shape/name.
expectations.evidence: The assistant proceeded to implement the requested handler directly and did not ask which framework, path, shape, or function name to use.

expectations.label: Turn 2 should not ask for comment placement, tone, or wording clarification.
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 directly added the one-line comment and did not ask where or how to place/word it before doing so.

expectations.label: The assistant should not stall with a proposed-change style flow for this in-repo file edit.
expectations.evidence: The assistant delivered concrete edits ('Added ...', 'Done — added ...') and did not present a `## Proposed change` block.

samples.notes: All expectations were met with direct execution in both turns and no blocking clarification pattern. The only minor ambiguity is that the transcript only proves reading `middleware.ts` (not `auth.ts`), but that was not required to block scoring in the scenario's acceptance logic. Overall, the behavior aligns with execute-first expectations.

scenarios.title: Agent commits to execution rather than interrupting with a clarifying question

expectations.label: Use the edit tool to make the requested code change
expectations.evidence: Tools invoked include `edit`; the transcript also shows `codebase/src/healthz.ts` was written.

expectations.label: Read `codebase/src/middleware.ts` before writing to mirror existing convention
expectations.evidence: Files read include `codebase/src/middleware.ts`, matching the user’s invitation to inspect existing style.

expectations.label: Create `codebase/src/healthz.ts` in turn 1 with TypeScript named export, JSDoc header, and `{ status: "ok" }` response without asking the user to confirm design choices
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 assistant message states it added `codebase/src/healthz.ts` with a named export, JSDoc on top, request parameter, and fixed `return { status: "ok" };`.

expectations.label: In turn 2, add a one-line comment above the handler (additive edit)
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 assistant says: “Done — I added a one-line comment above the handler:” and shows the inline comment above `export function healthz`.

expectations.label: If assumptions are mentioned, state them narratively rather than as blocking questions
expectations.evidence: Across both turns, the agent did not stop to ask the user to choose names/shapes and reported completed actions directly.

expectations.label: Do not invoke any clarifying-question/ask-the-user tool in turn 1
expectations.evidence: Tool list contains only `edit`, `grep`, and `read`; no `AskQuestion`/`ask`/`clarify` tool was used.

expectations.label: Do not invoke any clarifying-question tool in turn 2
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 response contains only completion text and code, with no clarify-type tool invocation.

expectations.label: Do not end turn 1 with a question that blocks execution
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 concludes with “Added ...” and a code block, no blocking question is present.

expectations.label: Do not stall in turn 1 to ask for HTTP framework, path, shape, or function name
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 directly created `healthz.ts` and did not ask for those unresolved choices.

expectations.label: Do not ask in turn 2 for comment placement, tone, or wording
expectations.evidence: Turn 2 directly implements the one-line comment and does not ask follow-up questions.

expectations.label: Do not misclassify this code edit as a proposed-only structural change
expectations.evidence: No `Proposed change` block appears; the assistant directly produced and updated code.

samples.notes: All listed expectations are satisfied by the transcript, including direct execution and no clarifying-question interruptions.
